meta.title: AI Hallucination Cases in Legal Proceedings
meta.description: A research dataset of documented AI hallucination incidents from real court proceedings. Includes cases where generative AI produced fabricated citations, invented quotes, or misrepresented legal authorities.
meta.citation: Kameir, Christian. AI Hallucination Cases in Legal Proceedings. Decentralized Publishing LLC. https://kameir.com/AI-legal-research

cases.name: Tekoma Chaney v. Transdev Services Inc. et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Attributed 'In re Shubert, 799 F.3d 1124, 1129 (9th Cir. 2015)' as authority in Plaintiff's Opposition to Ogilvie's MSJ (p.17); Court found the case fabricated and the reporter/pincite corresponds to an unrelated real case.
Same fabricated citation used twice in Plaintiff's Opposition to Transdev's MSJ (Opp'n TMSJ pp.15–16); Court noted counsel failed to disclose these additional uses in initial declaration.
Third instance of the same fabricated authority appearing in briefing; Court treated repeated uses as aggravating factor in finding bad faith and imposing monetary and reporting sanctions.

cases.outcome: Monetary Sanction; Reporting to other courts

cases.name: Brandon Rulund Akins v. Tashi Latwon Ratliff, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited two federal decisions with docket numbers and Westlaw citations that the court reports do not exist as cited.
Plaintiff cited a non-existent case at the given citation; court found the citation does not exist as cited.
Plaintiff relied on a cited North Carolina appellate case that the court found not to exist at the cited authority.
Plaintiff cited this state-court authority at an incorrect/nonexistent citation; court flagged it as inaccurate.
Federal case citation in plaintiff's filings does not exist at the cited report; court identified it as a fabricated or inaccurate citation.
Plaintiff cited an M.D.N.C. decision and citation that the court states does not exist at that citation.
Plaintiff referenced a case name without identifying information; court treated the unsupported citation as inaccurate.

Plaintiff asserted the existence of a 2024 North Carolina Supreme Court COVID-related decision supporting insurer liability; court found that characterization inaccurate and the cited authority incorrect.
Plaintiff relied on a docket citation and asserted procedural facts about that case (e.g., pro se amendment) that the court found inaccurate or unsupported by the cited docket.

cases.name: Anthony C. Hill v. Workday, Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Filed brief included a citation to a nonexistent case; court found the citation fabricated and tied its inclusion in part to AI use.
Court found the brief misrepresented the holding of a real case cited by counsel.

cases.outcome: Admonishment; Monetary Fine; 4 hours live CLE; Order Circulation

cases.name: Laurie Ibach and Mark Stewart v. Bruce Stewart

hallucinationItems.description: Cited a non-existent Nebraska Court of Appeals decision presented as on‑point; Court found the citation actually pointed to unrelated Iowa criminal cases.
Cited a purported Alabama Civil Appellate opinion that does not exist.
Cited an apparently fictitious Alabama case used to support tolling/estoppel arguments.
Attributed a quotation to Ex parte Helms that does not appear in that opinion; Court found the quoted language absent from Helms.
Attributed language to Ex parte Seabol regarding fiduciary concealment tolling the statute that the Court found is not in the cited pages of Seabol.
Cited a non-existent Alabama appellate decision repeatedly to support estoppel/limitations arguments.
Quoted Janowiak v. Tiesi as supporting a proposition; Court found the quoted material not present and the reporter/pinpoint citations mismatched.
Cited an apparently fabricated Illinois appellate opinion purportedly imposing constructive trust for undue influence; Court found no case matching the citation/style.

cases.outcome: Adverse Costs Order; Doubled Costs; Filing Prohibition; Bar Referral

cases.name: Joann Crawford v. Buffalo Creek Properties, LLC

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant cited 'In re Estate of Denman' at 462 S.W.3d 641 (Tex. App.—Austin 2015, no pet.); the court found this citation incorrect/misplaced (page cite corresponds to an Arkansas criminal case) and that Texas decisions titled In re Denman have different cites.
Appellant referenced 'Hercenberger v. Proctor' without a reporter citation; the court found no binding authority by that name and no supporting citation.

Appellant cited a study titled 'Underestimating the Unrepresented: Cognitive Biases Disadvantage Pro Se Litigants in Family Law Cases'; the court indicated it could find no record of a binding authority or known publication by that name.

cases.name: State of Israel v. Vakrat
Primerica Life Insurance Company v. Daniel Vett Finlayson; Michelle Johnson

hallucinationItems.description: Opposition contained multiple case citations the Court could not locate on Westlaw; counsel later confirmed clerical/research errors and the Court suggested possible improper AI use.
Opposition included quotations attributed to cases that the Court could not verify; the quotations were inaccurate or unlocatable.

cases.name: Meyer v. Castroville State Bank

hallucinationItems.description: Appellants cited an apparent Texas Supreme Court opinion that does not exist; the Bank highlighted the issue and the court confirmed the authority is fictitious.
Appellants cited a non-existent opinion purportedly from the Corpus Christi court; the Bank pointed it out and the court's research verified it does not exist.

cases.name: Alishia Monique Jones v. Ahmed Taylor, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Court found the citation to a case titled Tokio Marine & Fire Insurance Company v. Flota Mercante Grancolombiana, S.A., 35 F.3d 1134 (6th Cir. 1994) is not a real case and likely AI-generated; court warned that citing fake cases is sanctionable.
Court noted United States v. Robinson, 305 F.3d 732 (6th Cir. 2002) appears to be a fabricated citation generated by AI and warned the plaintiff may be sanctioned for continued use of fake citations.

cases.name: Carol L. Williams v. Tracy Honl

hallucinationItems.description: Brief cited 'Tubra v. Cooke, 233 Or App 339, 225 P3d 862 (2010)' for an anti‑SLAPP proposition; court found the cited case does not exist.
Brief attributed de novo‑review language and standards to Neumann v. Liles; court found Neumann does not state the claimed proposition.
Brief quoted Staten v. Steel as supporting a broad rule that private employment disputes are not matters of public interest; court found Staten does not say that.
Brief relied on Davoodian v. Rivera and similar citations for propositions or quotations the court identified as omitted or unsupported; listed among problematic citations.
Brief invoked Handy v. Lane for a proposition about government transparency and accountability that the court found Handy did not contain or emphasize as claimed.

cases.name: Krista C. Geddes v. LoanCare, LLC, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Court found plaintiff misquoted and enlarged the scope of Kachlon; the quoted language does not appear in the opinion and was used to support removing nominal status for trustees.
Court found the Barrionuevo quotation was nonexistent or unrelated to the cited opinion; Barrionuevo addresses the tender rule and does not discuss nominal party status.

cases.name: ARIH Québec c. Santé Québec

hallucinationItems.description: Arbitral reliance on an alleged arbitral decision 'Arbitrage CHU Ste‑Justine (D.T.E. 2018-30)'; SOQUIJ and the Court confirmed this arbitral decision does not exist.
Arbitral footnote citing a doctrinal piece attributed to Frédéric Bachand; Court found the referenced article/book chapter introuvable and not substantiating the tribunal's point.

Arbitral citation to a Court of Appeal decision listed as supporting contractual time‑limits; Court found the cited 'Ville de Montréal c. Syndicat des cols bleus regroupés de Montréal, 2005 QCCA 591' to be nonexistent/the neutral reference refers to another case.
Arbitral reference to 'Groleau et Groupe Pages Jaunes Cie, 2011 QCCS 5386' which the Court determined does not exist and the citation number leads to a different decision.
Arbitral citation to 'Tremblay c. Commission scolaire de la Jonquière, 2002 CanLII 24357 (QCCA)' found by the Court to be nonexistent/the neutral reference points elsewhere.

cases.outcome: Arbitral award annulled because the award relied on nonexistent ('hallucinated') authorities.

cases.name: Boonstra v Edmonton (City)
cases.court: Court of King’s Bench of Alberta

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant's written submissions included at least 13 case-law citations generated by ChatGPT that the City identified as non-existent; the Court admonished the appellant and disregarded submissions based on those authorities.

cases.name: Bunce v. Visual Technology Innovations (2)

hallucinationItems.description: At least one cited authority in Rajan's omnibus motion was made up (AI-generated); the Court found a made-up case among his citations.
Multiple cited authorities were erroneous or did not support the propositions for which they were cited; Court found Rajan failed to verify these AI-generated citations.

cases.name: Arthur West v. Lower Duwamish Waterway Group

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant provided fictitious quotations purportedly from existing cases; court found these quotations to be invented and rebuked the lack of candor.
Appellant included invented or 'hallucinated' citations in his briefing; court noted these citations were fabricated or generated by AI or other sources.

cases.name: In re Prince Global Holdings Limited, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: AI-generated, non-existent/incorrect Westlaw citation for Three Arrows Capital appearing in the Motion; corrected in Schedule A.
Garbled/doubled citation for Soundview Elite (mixed volumes, pages, and years) in the Motion; corrected in Schedule A.
Inaccurate quotation/misstatement attributed to Soundview Elite in the Motion (garbled language and tense); corrected in Schedule A.
Clearly erroneous citation with impossible year/number formatting for Three Arrows/related filings (e.g., year '20234'); corrected in Schedule A.

cases.name: Jamie Lee Saunders v. Albertsons/Safeway, LLC

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff's reply brief relied on a fictional, AI-generated case; opposing counsel raised the issue in a surreply; plaintiff admitted the error and apologized; court declined to strike the motion but issued a warning about future hallucinated citations.

cases.name: Essandoh v. Capital One Bank
Amit Arora v Canadian National Railway

hallucinationItems.description: Applicant relied on generative AI resulting in citations to non-existent jurisprudence and misstatements of the law; the AJ identified reliance on non-existent cases in the Applicant's submissions.
Applicant miscited and misrepresented the propositions of real cases in submissions (inaccurate reliance on jurisprudence), identified by the Court as misleading and unverified AI-generated material.

cases.name: Fofanah v. Rockwall Rental Properties

hallucinationItems.description: Appellants relied on Khan v. GBAK Properties, Inc. for a notice-of-default proposition the opinion does not support; the court noted the citation does not stand for the asserted proposition.
Multiple other opinions cited in appellants' reply brief did not support the propositions for which they were offered; the court noted at least four such misstatements.
Appellants cited 'Jones v. American Real Estate Services, LLC' as an opinion of this court; extensive searches found no such opinion and the court described the citation as hallucinated.

cases.name: Matter of: LOGMET LLC, B-423066.2

hallucinationItems.description: Protester cited 'Innovative Test Asset Solutions, LLC, B-419132, Dec. 16, 2020, 2020 CPD ¶ 398', a decision GAO could not locate and treated as non-existent.
Protester misrepresented the content of several decisions in its filings; GAO noted inaccuracies and the protester later acknowledged certain citations were 'inaccurate'.

cases.name: In the Marriage of: Sarah Lynn Nelson

hallucinationItems.description: Husband cited 'DeMars v. DeMars, 590 N.W.2d 116, 120-21 (Minn. Ct. App. 1999)', which the court was unable to locate and flagged as an invalid/nonexistent citation, noting AI-generated hallucinations as a known source of such fake cases.

cases.name: Stance Strong and Kinisha Strong v. Zubha Pop Foods LLC d/b/a Popeyes Chicken

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiffs cited Dunn v. Old Orchard Ctr., Ltd., 306 Ill. App. 3d 75 (1999) for a proposition the actual case (People v. Maurice Dunn) does not support; court identified the citation as misattributed/misrepresented.

Plaintiffs cited Arnold v. Hoyt, 384 F. Supp. 566 (1974) and quoted language not found in the actual opinion; court notes the citation corresponds to Geldermann & Co., Inc. v. Dussault and the quoted passage is absent.

Court and defendant identified numerous non-existent or fictitious cases in plaintiffs' briefs (the motion alleges over twenty fake cases), including an uncited/reference-only 'Fausett v. Walgreen Co' with no supporting citation or record.

cases.name: H.B. v. Conseil des écoles publiques de l’Est de l’Ontario

hallucinationItems.description: Applicant cited 'Shah v. Toronto DSB, 2021 HRTO 342'; tribunal could not retrieve that case and found the citation corresponded to a different decision (Awad v Petrakos), concluding the reference was AI-generated fake case law.
Applicant referred to 'Rule 25.11'; tribunal noted no such rule exists in the Human Rights Code or Tribunal Rules and that reference was false/non-existent.

cases.outcome: Ordered applicant to provide digital copies of all case law they intend to rely on

cases.name: Langen v. The Owners, Strata Plan EPS5455

hallucinationItems.description: Applicants summarized several existing cases incorrectly; tribunal found the summaries did not stand for the legal bases advanced and were likely AI-generated misrepresentations.
Applicants included a fabricated/false quote attributed to one case; tribunal identified the quote as fake and likely produced by AI.

cases.name: Brian Smith v. TD Auto Finance LLC, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Non-signatory defendants alerted the court that Smith's motion cited AI-hallucinated (fictitious) cases; the court noted prior admonishment for fictitious citations.

cases.name: Michael T-Alexander v. Michelle Baass et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant included purported quotations attributed to Tennessee v. Lane that do not appear in that opinion; court found the quoted language fabricated.
Appellant cited People v. Superior Court (Humberto S.) for a quotation that does not appear in that case; court could not locate the quoted language in any authority.
Multiple citation references in appellant's briefs were incorrect or referred to non-existent authorities; editor's note preserved invalid citations in the official record.

cases.name: Zand v. Sukumar
Shaw v. City of Portola, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff used an incorrect pinpoint citation to People v. Dang, citing page 1305 though the published opinion ends at page 1300, and relied on Dang for a privilege proposition that is inapplicable; court identified the pin‑cite and inapposite reliance.
Plaintiff repeatedly cited a nonexistent case, Boyd v. City of San Diego, 14 Cal.App.4th 954, 959-960 (1993); court found no such published authority and treated it as a fictitious citation.

Plaintiff cited In re Grand Jury Subpoena as a Ninth Circuit decision (In re Grand Jury Subpoena (Under Seal) (9th Cir. 1985) 774 F.2d 624), but the opinion is a Fourth Circuit case; court noted the misattribution.

Plaintiff initially cited State of Nevada v. US Dept. of Energy with incorrect reporter information (151 F.3d 1236, 1242); plaintiff later corrected to Nevada v. United States Dep't of Energy, 133 F.3d 1201 (9th Cir. 1998); court accepted correction.

Plaintiff cited In re County of Los Angeles, 223 F.3d 990 (9th Cir. 2000) as supporting an assertion about federal courts' inherent authority, but the court found no explicit passage in that opinion supporting the claimed proposition.

Plaintiff relied on Larson v. Domestic & Foreign Commerce Corp., 337 U.S. 682 (1949) to support the proposition that courts void or enjoin ultra vires public spending; court found Larson only mentions ultra vires and does not support that spending‑enjoining proposition.

cases.name: Cynthia White v. Walmart, Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: AI-generated list asserted that Defendant's answer to every interrogatory was deficient; court found counsel relied exclusively on AI and failed to vet those asserted deficiencies.

AI-produced 'talking points' identifying discovery deficiencies were copied/pasted into an email and used in lieu of a genuine meet-and-confer; court held this did not satisfy meet-and-confer obligations.

cases.name: State of Ohio v. Abdullah M. Alqahtani

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant's brief included at least one cited case that the court found "do[es] not seem to exist." Court characterized this as fabricated authority.
Appellant relied on cases that the court found misapplied or irrelevant to the radar/BEE III manual arguments.
Appellant incorrectly cited R.C. 4511.091 as the source for a calibration/proof rule; court corrected this misstatement of the law.

cases.name: Allen v. Hunt

hallucinationItems.description: Court and Magistrate Judge noted prior filings containing false citations and quotes; Judge Kendall previously found filings with citations to nonexistent cases and quotes from no real case and imposed a $1,500 Rule 11 sanction in a separate matter.

Plaintiff cited "Sterigenics U.S., LLC v. Sotera Health Co., 2020 WL 5645683 (N.D. Ill. Sep. 22, 2020)", which the Court found does not exist; plaintiff admitted the citation was fabricated and filed a notice of correction.

cases.name: Avalon Holdings Corp. v. Guy Gentile

hallucinationItems.description: Gentile cited a non-existent Second Circuit opinion and attributed a quotation to it; the court could not locate any such opinion or the quoted language and concluded the citation was fictitious, possibly produced by generative AI.

cases.name: Steven Nemec v. Kelsey Kanoelani Alao and Alyssa Jade Alao

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited a non-existent case; the court found Hernandez v. Cnty. of Yamhill (2017) does not exist and treated it as an AI-generated fabricated citation.
Plaintiff cited a non-existent case; the court found U.S. Bank Nat’l Ass’n v. Taylor (2015) does not exist and treated it as an AI-generated fabricated citation.
Plaintiff cited a non-existent case; the court found Johns v. County of Douglas (2013) does not exist and treated it as an AI-generated fabricated citation.
Plaintiff cited a non-existent case; the court found McNeil v. United States (2015) does not exist and treated it as an AI-generated fabricated citation.

cases.name: Bruno Roberto Rodriguez v. Kathryn Louise Rodriguez

hallucinationItems.description: Petition included citations to multiple cases that do not exist; court concluded some cited cases were fabricated.
Petition cited actual cases but attributed legal propositions to them that the cases do not support; court found these citations mischaracterized precedent.

cases.name: That Xiong v. Minga Wofford

hallucinationItems.description: Petitioner's brief cited 'G.A.A. v. Chestnut, No. 1:25-cv-01102-EPG, 2025 WL 3030589 (E.D. Cal. Nov. 21, 2025)'; Court found the Westlaw citation corresponded to a different real case, indicating a hallucinated citation likely from GenAI.

Court previously identified a citation in Petitioner's reply to 'Phan v. Barr, No. 1:19-cv-01451, 2019 WL 7758773 (E.D. Cal. Dec. 20, 2019)' that it could not locate and which appeared to be hallucinated.

Court previously identified a citation in Petitioner's reply to 'Flores v. Barr, No. 1:20-cv-00491, 2020 WL 1939565 (E.D. Cal. Apr. 22, 2020)' that it could not locate and which appeared to be hallucinated.

cases.name: Harry v. Arie Jan
Ifeoma Delliane Chinedu Obi v. Cook County, Illinois, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff quoted language attributed to Marshall v. Marshall (547 U.S. 293 (2006)) that the Court found does not appear in that decision.
Plaintiff relied on a non-existent Seventh Circuit case cited as Andrade v. Arby Concessions to dispute the court's dismissal; Court found the case is not real.
Plaintiff attributed a quoted passage to this Court's November 18, 2025 Order that the Court found is not in that Order.
Plaintiff quoted Ridder v. City of Springfield (109 F.3d 288) as saying sanctions are unavailable unless served before the case is disposed of; Court found that language is not in Ridder.

cases.name: In re Troylond Malon Wise

hallucinationItems.description: Supplemental Response cited La. R.S. § 9:5774, a statute that does not exist; counsel acknowledged the citation was incorrect and should have cited La. Civ. Code art. 3357 et seq.

Cited 'In re Garcia' without a complete citation; attorney admitted the intended Garcia case "does not support the precise proposition for which it was cited," and the court found this to be an unverified AI-generated reference that rendered the pleading misleading.
Supplemental Response cited 'In re Buttermilk Towne LLC,' which the court determined 'does not resolve to any opinion' (AI-generated case citation that does not appear to exist).

cases.outcome: Monetary sanction; CLE on AI; barred until compliance; Brief struck

cases.name: M. [W] [Y] c. CPAM du Loiret

hallucinationItems.description: The claimant cited 'jurisprudences purement fantaisiste' (manifestly invented case law) to argue a defect of motivation; the court characterized these citations as invented and akin to an AI hallucination and did not rely on them.

cases.name: Robert S. Hulvat v. Juli Gumina, STG Divorce Law, and Jennifer B. Hulvat

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant's brief cited multiple nonexistent cases to support legal arguments (nine identified fictitious authorities). Court described these as fictitious AI-generated citations.

Appellant quoted language allegedly from Krumwiede to support a rule on dismissal with prejudice; the quoted language does not appear in the actual decision and the cited Krumwiede citation was incorrect.
Appellant attributed a holding concerning 'fraud upon the court' to In re Marriage of Lehr, which the court determined was a misrepresentation of that case's holding.

cases.name: Joseph C. Zoghaib v. Societe Generale De Banque Au Liban, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited Meier ex rel. Meier to argue FSIA does not bar jurisdictional discovery; Court observed Meier concerns personal jurisdiction and does not reference FSIA, immunity, or discovery stays.

Plaintiff relied on In re Clerici as support for jurisdictional discovery/FSIA-related propositions; Court noted In re Clerici addresses 28 U.S.C. § 1782 and does not discuss FSIA, immunity, or stays of discovery.

Plaintiff cited McCarthy v. Barnett Bank of Polk Cnty. as establishing the standard for staying discovery; Court explained McCarthy concerns media intervention/confidentiality and does not address motions to stay discovery.

cases.name: Pleasant v. Burns

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited at least one case that does not exist; the court identified the citation as fabricated and required explanation but Plaintiff did not address it.
Plaintiff repeatedly misstated the holdings of existing cases (unsupported statements of authority); court listed these misrepresentations in the show-cause order.
Plaintiff quoted language attributed to cases that the court found do not exist (fabricated quotations); court ordered Plaintiff to explain the origin of the quoted language.

cases.name: James C. Young; Tahnee R. Young v. Happy Money

hallucinationItems.description: Debtors cited "In re Pringle, 377 B.R. 308 (Bankr. D. Colo. 2007)"; court found the citation does not exist as cited and a search returns an unrelated New Mexico case.

Debtors cited "In re Johnson, 276 B.R. 367 (Bankr. E.D. Va. 2002)"; court found the citation as cited does not exist and the case retrieved from database was a Delaware opinion unrelated to the issues.

Debtors cited "In re Steward, 475 B.R. 518 (Bankr. E.D. Mo. 2012)"; court's search retrieved Swanson v. Applied Process Technology Int'l, LLC (In re Delta-T Corp.), 475 B.R. 495 (Bankr. E.D. Va. 2012), showing the cited page and case do not match Debtors' description.

Debtors cited "In re Cherrett, 873 F.3d 1060 (9th Cir. 2017)" and relied on it for a sanctions-related proposition; court found the retrieved Cherrett case exists but concerns a different issue (consumer/business debt), so Debtors mischaracterized the case's holding.

cases.name: Aurore Sims v. Assia Souily-Lefave; Around Vegas, LLC

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited Cruz v. Fox for the proposition regarding premature depositions; the Court found the citation to be AI-generated/misleading and not supporting the claimed proposition.
Plaintiff cited Green, Tweed of Delaware, Inc. v. DuPont Dow Elastomers, L.L.C. as addressing assertion of privilege; the Court treated the citation as AI-generated or otherwise unreliable.

Plaintiff cited Barrow v. Greenville Indep. Sch. Dist. as allegedly granting a protective order when a deposition was premature; the Court found the citation to be AI-generated/mischaracterized and not reliable for that proposition.

Plaintiff cited Pioneer Drive, LLC v. Nissan Diesel America, Inc. and characterized it as supporting prematurity arguments; the Court noted that the cited opinion says nothing about prematurity and treated Plaintiff's reliance as AI-generated/misleading (but acknowledged the opinion recognizes Rule 37's flexibility).

cases.name: Thanh Nguy v. Jabil Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Original opposition included a repeatedly-cited, purported E.D. Cal. decision that the court found likely to be a fictitious AI-generated case; counsel later filed a corrected brief removing the citation and the court declined to sanction.

cases.name: Herbert Brooks v. Lowes Home Centers LLC

hallucinationItems.description: Counsel cited Tompkins v. Cyr for a rule about prior settlements/verdicts being irrelevant; the court could not find that language or the referenced topic in Tompkins.
Counsel relied on Collins v. Wayne Corp. to support the same proposition about prior settlements; the court found Collins unrelated to that issue.
Brief quoted 'the policy behind Rule 408 is to encourage settlements' and attributed it to Bankcard America; the court found Bankcard America uses 'purpose' not the quoted wording.
Counsel attributed the phrase 'must have acted in "bad faith"' to King v. Illinois Central R.R.; the court found the phrase is not present in the cited opinion.
Counsel quoted language about surgery and spoliation attributed to Gilliam v. Uni Holdings; the court found the quoted sentences do not appear at the cited pincite and the correct pages differ.
Brief quoted 'did not have a duty to delay his surgery' and cited Menges v. Cliffs Drilling Co.; the court found that quoted phrase does not appear in Menges at the cited location.

Brief quoted a sentence about the 'tortfeasor tak[ing] his victim as he finds him' and cited Lasha v. Olin Corp.; the court found the opinion uses 'defendant' (not 'tortfeasor') and the pincite was incorrect.

cases.name: Blackburn v. United States

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited Davoll, 194 F.3d 1116, 1138 for the proposition that Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(a)(2)(C) limits treating-physician opinion testimony as claimed; court found the pinpointed pages discuss lay-witness scope, not non-retained expert testimony — mischaracterization.

Plaintiff cited Muscogee (Creek) Nation, 611 F.3d 1222, 1237 as holding treating physicians may not give opinions formed in anticipation of litigation; court found that opinion does not discuss treating-physician testimony — misrepresentation.

Plaintiff cited 'Hall v. United States, 2018 WL 1620923 (D. Colo. Apr. 3, 2018)' to support a proposition, but the provided Westlaw identifier directed to Poppino v. Berryhill, an unrelated decision that does not support the quote — a miscitation/fabricated support.

Plaintiff cited Davoll, 194 F.3d 1116, 1138 (10th Cir. 1999) for the proposition that treating-physician opinions are limited under Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(a)(2)(C); court noted the cited pages address a treating physician as a lay witness and do not support Plaintiff's argument.

Plaintiff cited Muscogee (Creek) Nation, 611 F.3d 1222, 1237 (10th Cir. 2010) as holding treating physicians may not testify to opinions developed in anticipation of litigation; court found that opinion does not discuss treating physicians.

Plaintiff cited Hall v. United States, 2018 WL 1620923 (D. Colo. Apr. 3, 2018) for a proposition about VA doctors; court observed the Westlaw identifier led to Poppino v. Berryhill (D.N.M.), which is unrelated and does not support the quoted proposition.

cases.name: RvV X / IX

hallucinationItems.description: Counsel attached a self-made translated copy of the transfer decision (Annexe 26quater) that omitted the signatory's official function; the Council described the document as apparently produced with the help of artificial intelligence and misleading.

cases.name: Nancy Parkinson v. Unemployment Compensation Board of Review

hallucinationItems.description: Claimant cited a non-existent case to support her argument that premature recoupment of benefits was contrary to Pennsylvania precedent; the Court noted the citation does not exist and declined to consider the argument.

cases.name: Viral Bhagat v. Anuja Sharad Shah

hallucinationItems.description: Defendant cited "In re SunEdison, Inc. Sec. Litig., 314 F.R.D. 139, 143 (S.D.N.Y. 2016)"; the court could not locate the case or quoted text on docket or Westlaw and concluded the citation was an AI-generated fabrication.

cases.name: James Jordan v. USA, Clinicas Del Camino Real, Inc., et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff attributes a direct quotation to City of Chicago v. International College of Surgeons (522 U.S. 156) that does not appear in that opinion; plaintiff also mis-cited the case name order.

Plaintiff cites Nevada Power Co. v. Monsanto Co., 151 F.3d 1201, 1205 (9th Cir. 1998); the reporter/pincite (151 F.3d 1201) actually corresponds to McKeon v. United States, and Nevada Power is at a different reporter citation—court calls this an "apparently fake citation."

cases.name: Wendy Wolkenstein and Rebecca Knepp v. Dan Heinrich and Samual Tillery

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiffs cited Stanley v. University of Southern California for the proposition that service-of-process fees are taxable under 28 U.S.C. § 1920(1); court found the citation does not support that proposition.
Plaintiffs cited Shum v. Intel Corp. as Ninth Circuit authority that pro se litigants may recover reasonable costs; court noted Shum is a Federal Circuit case and does not say what plaintiffs claim.

Plaintiffs cited a Boilermaker v. Skelly decision with a 2013 WL citation; court found no such cited decision exists and that the actual Boilermaker opinion (389 F. Supp. 2d 1222) does not say what plaintiffs claimed.

cases.name: Kelly Bartholomew v. Public Utility Commission of Oregon

hallucinationItems.description: Fabricated cases were used to support multiple incorrect legal propositions and each of petitioner's three assignments of error relied on fabricated authority.
Opening brief included at least 10 fabricated case citations identified by PUC; the court found fabricated legal authority permeated the brief.

Specific fabricated citation cited in the brief: 'McKay Creek Valley Ass’n v. Washington County, 309 Or App 112, 124-25, 482 P3d 827 (2021)'; petitioner later claimed she intended a different, real McKay Creek Valley case.

cases.outcome: Judicial review dismissed due to submission of fabricated case citations

cases.name: Gamez v. County of Fresno

hallucinationItems.description: Brief cites a case the Court could not locate; appears to be a nonexistent citation.
Brief cites a case the Court could not locate; appears to be a nonexistent citation.
Brief cites a case the Court could not locate; appears to be a nonexistent citation.
Brief attributes a specific quoted passage to Destfino that the Court could not find on the cited page.
Brief asserts a legal rule regarding Rule 81(c)(2) without citation; Court found the representation unsupported and contrary to established authority.

cases.name: Hardy v. Genesee County Community Action Resource Department, et al. (2)

hallucinationItems.description: Court found the quoted phrase 'reason to believe' attributed to Berndt v. Tennessee does not appear in that opinion and the case does not address dismissal of litigants with mental issues.
Court found the phrase 'amateur psychologist' attributed to United States v. Frazier does not appear in that opinion and the criminal case does not address the issues raised.
Court found the plaintiff's reference to '17 Quarters of Silence' as arising from Williams v. Reed is inaccurate; Williams contains no such phrase and did not address discovery disputes.

cases.name: In re: Philips Recalled CPAP, Bi-Level PAP, and Mechanical Ventilator Products Liability Litigation (Gravelyn)

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited Xarelto as In re Xarelto (Rivaroxaban) Prods. Liab. Litig., 223 F. Supp. 3d 1348, 1349 (J.P.M.L. 2016); the reporter citation provided is incorrect/for a different MDL and the holding was misrepresented.
Plaintiff cited Eliquis (Apixaban) as supporting denial of transfer though the Panel in Eliquis created an MDL and transferred the actions; the holding was misrepresented.

Plaintiff cited In re Bard IVC Filters, 2016 WL 5867451 (J.P.M.L. Oct. 5, 2016), but that citation points to a Southern District of New York decision rather than a J.P.M.L. decision; authority misrepresented/miscited.

Plaintiff cited In re Vioxx, 478 F. Supp. 2d 1376 (J.P.M.L. 2007), but that citation leads to a Northern District of Georgia decision rather than the Panel decision cited; miscitation/misrepresentation.

cases.name: In re: Kathleen A Rabon

hallucinationItems.description: Debtor's counsel presented quoted language purportedly from a cited case that the Court found does not appear in the opinion; a second cited case likewise did not contain the quoted language or support the proposition.

cases.name: Modern Floor Specialists, Inc. et al. v. City of Los Angeles et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Counsel cited a purported Ninth Circuit case 'Mabee v. County of Orange, 784 F. App'x 585, 588 (9th Cir. 2019)'; the court found no such case and that the reporter/pincite instead corresponds to Cochran v. City of Wichita (10th Cir.). Court ordered counsel to produce the Mabee opinion or show cause.

cases.name: Ward v. Amazon.com Services LLC

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited 'Gile v. Dolgen Cal., LLC, 2023 WL 2669812, at *3 (9th Cir. 2023) (unpublished)'; Court could not locate a case at that citation and found a different Gile decision (2022 WL 17248087) that did not support Plaintiff's waiver argument.

Plaintiff cited 'Britton v. Co-op Banking Grp., 4 F.4th 742 (9th Cir. 2021)'; Court could not locate that citation, instead identifying older Britton decisions with different citations and holdings, concluding the cited authority was nonexistent or misrepresented.

cases.name: Kleyman Law Group, P.C. v. James Kaloidis

hallucinationItems.description: Court identified 'Feder Kaszovitz LLP v Rosen, 202 AD3d 421 [1st Dept 2022]' as a non-existent (fabricated) citation repeatedly used in Plaintiff's submissions.
Court identified 'Bauer v Cravath, Swain & Moore, LLP, 177 AD3d 479 [1st Dept 2019]' as a fictitious case cited by Plaintiff.
Court identified 'Pine v AJA Delivery, Inc., 201 AD3d 640 [2d Dept 2022]' (listed in filings as Pine v AIA Delivery) as a fabricated citation.
Plaintiff represented Pace v Raisman & Assocs. as dismissing counterclaims for post-breakdown claims; court found this to be a mischaracterization of the actual holding.
Plaintiff claimed Butler v Catinella involved sanctions; court found Butler does not involve sanctions and Plaintiff misstated that holding.
Court identified widespread misstatements of holdings (at least 83 instances) across many cited authorities, requiring judicial resources to correct.

cases.name: Ellen Michaelis v. Wilmington Savings Fund Society

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff attributes the statement "A void decree is open to collateral attack at any time." to Matter of Sakow (97 NY2d 436), but the quoted language does not appear in that case; court identified this as a false quotation.
Plaintiff cites "Kruger v. Kruger, 107 AD3d 1285, 1287 [3d Dept. 2013]", a case that does not exist; the reporter citation corresponds to Matter of Blocker v. Fischer, 107 AD3d 1285.
Court found that numerous additional citations in Plaintiff's affirmation were fabricated or misrepresented by GenAI, pointing to different cases, irrelevant cases, or no case at all.

cases.name: United States v. Farris

hallucinationItems.description: AI-generated quoted commentary attributed to U.S.S.G. § 3B1.1 cmt. n.1 did not appear in the cited commentary; the court could not locate similar language in any authority.

Brief attributed a quotation to United States v. Washington ('simply facilitating the offense without exercising decision-making authority is insufficient') that does not appear in that opinion; the court noted Washington in fact upheld the enhancement.

Brief attributed a quotation to United States v. Anthony ('[t]here was no evidence [the defendant] directed or supervised anyone else') that does not appear in the opinion; the court explained Anthony's vacatur was narrow and the defendant had conceded supervisory role.

cases.outcome: Counsel disqualified with no compensation for time served; Briefs locked; Bar Referral; Notice of Opinion

cases.name: Najafpir v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited multiple non-existent cases in the TAC which the Court concluded were hallucinated by AI and held non-existent cases cannot support his asserted legal propositions.

cases.name: AGC v Registrar of Land Titles
Stanford v. Leinart

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant cited a nonexistent opinion "Anderson v. Hood" in his brief; the court found the citation fabricated and likely AI-generated and admonished that citation of nonexistent cases is unacceptable.

cases.name: 9525-1260 Québec inc. c. Donato

hallucinationItems.description: Court found numerous AI-generated 'hallucinations juridiques' in the defendant's submitted documentation, consisting of erroneous legal interpretations and purported authorities; the court relied on this finding in discrediting the defendant's position.

cases.outcome: No weight given to AI-generated submissions

cases.name: Kevin D. Turnage v. Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff purports to quote 29 C.F.R. § 1614.110(b) for a 180-day decision requirement, but that language does not appear; court notes the correct provision is likely 29 C.F.R. § 1614.106(e)(2).
Plaintiff cited Gao v. United States, 2019 WL 11199934 (D. Ariz. Oct. 23, 2019); the Court was unable to locate this case.
Plaintiff cited Miller v. United States, 2009 WL 1532834 (D. Ariz. Apr. 30, 2009); the Court was unable to locate this case.
Plaintiff relied on 'Yomi v. United States Dept. of Health & Human Services, 970 8754 (D. Kan. 2022),' which the Court could not locate and treated as a fictitious citation.
Court observed the plaintiff cited additional unlocatable or fictitious authorities beyond those specifically identified.

cases.name: Patrick Hrdlichka v. Samantha Bengston

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant's brief cited multiple non-existent cases; the court searched Arkansas Reports, S.W. Reporter, and databases and found the authorities were fictitious, relying on these fabricated citations in his arguments.

cases.name: Saqib Kafeel v. Apple Inc., et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Reply cites a non-existent Ninth Circuit case; court notes the citation does not exist.

cases.name: In the Interest of W.G., Minor Child

hallucinationItems.description: AI attributed specific propositions of law to a cited case where those propositions did not appear (misattribution of holdings/authority).
Petition included at least one citation to a nonexistent Iowa appellate decision; court found citation was fabricated and ordered disclosure.

cases.name: Chelsea Montes v. Suns Legacy Partners LLC

hallucinationItems.description: Response to motion to dismiss included 'McIntyre v. Phx. Newspapers, Inc.' citation that was fabricated/mis-cited as filed.
Reply in support of anonymity contained multiple 'Doe v. ...' citations (e.g., Amazon) presented with Westlaw/West reporter info that did not exist as cited and were removed or corrected.

Reply included 'Doe v. Maricopa County Community College District, 2017 WL 4460441 (D. Ariz. Oct. 5, 2017)' that counsel could not identify as a real corresponding opinion; court treated it as fabricated.
Reply cited 'Doe v. Northrop Grumman Sys. Corp., 2022 WL 3447983 (E.D. Va. Aug. 17, 2022)' as authority; court found the citation was misreferenced/ fabricated as used.
Initial complaint cited a non-existent case and parenthetical about Equal Pay Act plausibility; court found the citation fictitious.
Response to motion to dismiss cited 'Pizzo v. City of Chandler' as a 2021 D. Ariz. opinion; court determined the citation was fabricated as presented.
Filings contained quoted phrases attributed to Ahanchian that did not appear verbatim in the opinion; court found they were paraphrased or presented as false quotations.
Filings included fabricated/direct-quotation-style phrases attributed to other real cases (e.g., Briones, Sibley, Valley Eng'rs) that were not direct quotes in those opinions.

cases.name: Joel A. Rivera v. Triad Properties Corporation, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: As-filed brief cited 'S.E.C. v. Digital Lightwave' as a D. Utah decision; court found no D. Utah opinion and that the actual M.D. Fla. case did not support the asserted proposition.
Brief attributed a specific quotation to Am. Fed'n of Lab. & Cong. of Indus. Orgs. v. City of Miami, but the quoted language does not appear in that opinion.

Plaintiffs cited Belcher v. Birmingham Tr. Nat'l Bank for a broad fiduciary-duty proposition; court found the cited page contained only headnotes addressing partnerships/corporations, not the asserted rule.
Plaintiffs relied on Jordan v. Mitchell to support an equitable-rule proposition; court explained Jordan did not address that issue and was distinguishable.
A quotation attributed to Ex parte Reindel appeared nowhere in that decision; the court determined the quoted passage was not found in Reindel.
Citation to '293 So. 3d 930' / 'Skinner v. Beemer' claimed to support notice-pleading; court found no such authority on point and that 293 So.3d 930 corresponds to Ex parte K.W., not notice pleading.

cases.outcome: Public Reprimand; Disqualification; Bar Referral; Publication and Notice of Order; Adverse Costs Order

cases.name: Ella and Ramin LLC v. Travelers Insurance Company

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited Harris v. One Hope United, Inc., 28 N.E.3d 804 (Ill. 2015) for the proposition that parent and subsidiary may be treated as a single entity, but the case concerns discovery privilege and does not support that corporate-law proposition (misapplied precedent).

Plaintiff repeatedly cited 'Heritage Common Partners v. Village of Summit' with 2009 Illinois reporter citations that the court could not locate; court found no Illinois case by that name and the only reported federal opinions by that name were from 1990–1991 and unrelated to the cited propositions.

cases.name: Evgeny Yaroshevsky v. Karlin et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff admitted using Google Gemini to obtain citations and failed to verify them before filing, contributing to the submission of fabricated and inaccurate authorities.

Multiple authorities cited by plaintiff did not exist; defendants identified at least five nonexistent cases in the complaint and five in other filings and the Court confirmed some citations were fabricated.
Numerous quotations attributed to cases could not be found in the opinions (Defendants identified ~13 instances; the Court found at least two such misquotes earlier).

cases.name: Zavadovsky v. Republic of Austria, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiffs cited Thomas v. Patton with a quotation the court found to be fabricated or falsely attributed.
Plaintiffs cited Young v. District of Columbia, 752 F. Supp. 2d 1 (D.D.C. 2010), which the court determined does not exist.
Plaintiffs attributed a fake quotation to Miller v. Fortune Ins. Co.; the court flagged the quotation as false.
Plaintiffs misrepresented the holding of Foremost-McKesson, Inc. v. Islamic Republic of Iran; the court noted the mischaracterization.
Plaintiffs misrepresented the holding of Wheat v. United States; the court observed the misrepresentation.
Plaintiffs attributed a fabricated quotation to United States v. Microsoft Corp.; the court found the quotation to be fake.
Plaintiffs cited a fabricated quotation attributed to Apotex, Inc. v. FDA; the court identified the quotation as fake.
Plaintiffs cited Beaucage v. Beaucage, 583 So.2d 846 (Fla. 5th DCA 1991), a case the court found to be nonexistent.

cases.name: Rosa Maria Cabrera Thompson v. Seattle Public Schools

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited a non-existent district-court decision as precedent; the court found the docket number cited corresponded to a different case and could not locate the cited authority on the district's docket, and ordered show-cause re sanctions.

cases.name: Staley v. City of Elba, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff's response (doc. 43 at 23) purports to quote and cite 'Kidd v. Mando Am. Corp., 731 F.3d 1196, 1200 (11th Cir. 2013)', which Defendants and the court identify as a non-existent citation.
Plaintiff cites 'Est. of Osorio v. Miami-Dade Cnty., 2023 WL 4003457 (S.D. Fla. June 15, 2023)' (doc. 43 at 24), which Defendants assert and the court notes does not exist.

Plaintiff's objection (doc. 49 at 28) cites 'Greer v. DeKalb County, 2009 WL 10101675, at *4 (N.D. Ga. July 13, 2009)', which Defendants identify and the court records as a citation that does not exist or is falsely stated.

cases.name: Federal Trade Commission v. James D. Noland, Jr., et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Court observed Individual Defendants’ filings contained hallucinated or otherwise inaccurate case citations (no specific fabricated citations listed in the order).

cases.name: Syreeta LaShawn McNeal v. Vera L. Calvin, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff repeatedly cited a non-existent opinion titled "Weniger v. Dynamic Recovery Solutions, LLC" with a referenced case number and Westlaw citation; the Court found no such case and noted the citation pointed to unrelated authorities.
Plaintiff cited Steen v. Murray, 919 F. Supp. 2d 993, 997 (S.D. Iowa 2013) as supporting a venue argument, but the Court found the citation mischaracterized the case's holding.

cases.name: Edward C. Allen v. Western Governors University, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff attributed the quote "both baseless and made without a reasonable and competent inquiry" to Christian v. Mattel; court found no such quote in that opinion.

Plaintiff attributed the quote "a reasonable attorney in like circumstances could believe his actions to be factually and legally justified" to Cooter & Gell; court found no such quote in that Supreme Court opinion.
Plaintiff attributed a leniency-for-pro-se quote to Warren v. Guelker, but the case contains no such quote and its holding is to the contrary; court found the attribution false.
Plaintiff attributed the quote "Rule 11 is not intended as a vehicle for intimidation or punishment for minor pleading errors" to Hudson; court found no such quote or holding in that opinion.
Plaintiff quoted the Supreme Court as cautioning against using Rule 11 "as a bar to the courthouse door" and cited Cooter & Gell at 393; court found no such quote in that opinion as used.
Plaintiff cited Sineneng-Smith and claimed it "acknowledg[ed] appellate errata" in a cited footnote; court found no such reference and the footnote irrelevant to plaintiff's position.

Plaintiff cited "Verinata Health, Inc. v. Ariosa Diagnostics, Inc., 2014 WL 10321141, at *2 (N.D. Cal. Nov. 21, 2014)" to support that errata are accepted; court found the Westlaw number incorrect/misleading and no such supporting authority.
Plaintiff cited Lewis v. Ryan (D. Ariz., Jan. 3, 2008) as accepting errata; court located a different Lewis v. Ryan under another number with no errata and found the representation false.

cases.outcome: Case dismissed with prejudice; Adverse Costs Order

cases.name: Hampton v. Lofton

hallucinationItems.description: Father's opening brief asserted trial-court findings (e.g., Child showed 'persistent hostility toward Father', 'difficulty adjusting to custodial transitions', Father's evidence was 'self-serving', Mother was 'evasive and inconsistent') that do not appear in the superior court's order; the appellate court described these as hallucinated.
Both Father's opening and reply briefs contained legal citations that the appellate court found either did not support his positions or 'appear to be fictitious', contrary to ARCAP 13 requirements.

cases.name: Elbert Coleman, III; Elbert Coleman, IV v. Nexo Capital Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiffs cited "Indiana Code § 23-2-1-2" for the proposition that transactions involving unregistered securities are void; the court found the statute has been repealed.
Plaintiffs cited "Kramer v. Trans-Lux Corp., 24 F.3d 1001 (7th Cir. 1994)"; the court found no such Seventh Circuit opinion and noted a District of Connecticut decision with that name instead.

cases.name: Feldman & Trost v. District 6 Board of Education

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiffs attributed the phrase "conduct of a sexual nature" to Rost v. Steamboat Springs RE-2 Sch. Dist.; the court found the case exists but the quoted language does not and the proposition cited was unsupported by the opinion.
Plaintiffs cited a Seventh Circuit decision that does not appear to exist, relying on it for a legal proposition.
Plaintiffs relied on Busse v. Motorola, Inc. for a theory of intrusion on seclusion, but the court found Busse inapposite and the reliance misplaced.

cases.name: Nguedi v. General Dynamics Information Technology

hallucinationItems.description: Attributed a nonexistent quotation to Brady v. Office of Sergeant at Arms; court found the quote does not appear in the decision and the case does not involve the stated proposition.
Attributed a nonexistent quotation and legal proposition to Figueroa v. Pompeo; court determined the quoted language and asserted topic do not appear in that decision.

Cited Doe v. United States Postal Service as containing a holding about the Workers’ Compensation Act’s exclusivity provision; court found the case contains no such quote and does not address that statute.
Mischaracterized Richardson v. United States as supporting a broad rule about granting leave to amend at late stages; court noted the cited opinion did not address that proposition.
Misstated the holding of Doe v. McMillan, citing it for a proposition the court says is the opposite of the actual holding.

cases.name: Jaii LeShawn Quinones v. Unknown Love, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited "Egelhoff v. Egelhoff, 628 F.2d 346" for the proposition that arbitrary passport restrictions are unconstitutional; the Court found the reporter cite fabricated and the asserted holding incorrect (real case is Egelhoff v. Egelhoff, 532 U.S. 141 (2001), which does not say that).
Plaintiff cited "Missouri ex rel. Wilkerson v. Wilkerson, 817 S.W.2d 77 (Mo. Ct. App. 1991)" and the Court found that citation does not exist as cited and does not support Plaintiff's proposition.
Plaintiff cited "Robinson v. Missouri Child Support Agency, 2021 WL 5437824 (Mo. Ct. App. 2021)" but the Court concluded the citation does not exist or does not support the asserted proposition.

Plaintiff attributed to Haig v. Agee the proposition that "government-imposed passport restrictions must be justified by compelling evidence;" the Court explained Plaintiff's characterization is the opposite of the case's actual holding.

cases.name: Leonard Colbert v. County of Riverside

hallucinationItems.description: Opposition included a quoted purported provision of Cal. Gov't Code § 845.4 that the court could not locate in the statute or in any case; court suggested counsel may have used AI and failed to confirm accuracy.
The opinion notes multiple citation references in the record that are incorrect or do not exist; Editor's Note removed links to those invalid citations.

cases.name: Heimkes v. Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condominium Owners Association, Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Relied on Chevron deference as authoritative despite Loper Bright having overruled its application; Court found Eaton misrepresented the status of the law.
Multiple citations in Eaton's filings were fabricated or did not exist; Court and Defendant found citations that either do not exist or do not match the referenced names.
Cited existing cases but attributed propositions or quotes those cases did not contain; Court's independent review found misrepresentations/false quotations of authorities.

cases.outcome: Reprimand; Order to file order in subsequent cases; Bar Referral; Adverse Costs Order

cases.name: Christopher A. Ambrose v. Bandy X. Lee

hallucinationItems.description: Cited Tyler v. Tyler to argue personal (in-hand) service is required in defamation actions; court found the citation inapposite and noted the misstatement.
Cited and relied on Martin v. Hearst Corp. and Hustler v. Falwell to support striking IIED claims; court found those authorities misread or inapplicable.
Court identified broader 'hallucinated legal propositions' in the briefing (unverified or severely misstated legal rules) and warned that such AI-assisted misstatements risk Rule 11 sanctions.

cases.name: Mohamed Hussain et al. v. Mansoor Quraishi et al.

hallucinationItems.description: The court identified multiple case citations in the brief that did not exist and labeled them as AI-generated/fabricated; court listed eight problematic citations in its order to show cause (Docket Entry 123).
The court found quotations attributed to cases in the brief that the court determined did not exist in the cited authorities (false quotes generated by AI).
The brief contained incorrect or misleading citations that misrepresented precedent or pointed to the wrong authorities; court noted citation errors in the order to show cause.

cases.name: Rushing v. Rushing et al.
cases.outcome: CA greenlighted dismissal of case for AI misuse

cases.name: Ford v. Sherwin-Williams

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff attributed a quotation to 'Clark v. City of Shawnee, Kansas, No. 21-2223-EFM, 2021 WL 4129476 (D. Kan. Sept. 9, 2021)', but the Westlaw number and case number pointed to unrelated authority; court concluded the citation/quotation was fictitious and likely AI-generated.

cases.name: Quandel Construction Group, Inc. v. Hunt Construction Group, Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Hunt cited a nonexistent Aerpio case and incorrect citation (wrong case name/number and Westlaw cite); court identified the correct case as Aerpio Pharms., Inc. v. Quaggin, 2019 WL 4717477 (S.D. Ohio Sept. 26, 2019).

Hunt included three direct quotations attributed to the (mis)cited Aerpio decision that do not appear in the actual Aerpio v. Quaggin opinion; court found only the phrase "inextricably intertwined" actually appears in the real case.
Hunt cited the wrong Vaughn case name and provided an unrelated case number; court found the error was wrong in form though the quoted substance was unchanged.

cases.name: Galang v. Canada (Attorney General)

hallucinationItems.description: Original Notice of Civil Claim contained references to non-existent court decisions appearing to be AI-generated; AGC raised the issue and Justice Dion ordered Part 3 struck and that any AI-assisted citations be verified prior to filing; plaintiff removed those references in amended pleadings.

cases.name: Danuta Dec v. Homeland Security

hallucinationItems.description: Opening brief included a false quotation (an imaginary quote) attributed in support of the standard of review; court found the quotation was inaccurate and likely AI-generated.
Opening brief cited a non-existent case (first instance); court identified it as a fabricated citation likely from AI and counsel could not explain its source.
Opening brief cited a second non-existent case; court treated this as another fabricated citation and noted counsel's inability to account for it.

cases.name: Shaun Fleck v. Del-One Federal Credit Union

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited 'FutureGrowth Corp. v. SEC, 955 F. Supp. 3d 441 (S.D.N.Y. 2025)'; the Court and defendant could not locate the case and the Court found the citation fabricated (Westlaw only showed Fleck's own filing).

cases.name: Yves Hector Vimegnon v. Gallagher Bassett Services, Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited 'Abbott v. Washington County,' which the Court could not locate and found to be a non-existent/mis-cited authority; Court warned that fabricated authority may justify sanctions.

cases.name: 76 Route 6 Holdings Inc. v. Town of Yorktown, NY

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff claimed McEachin v. McGuinnis contains language applying 'special solicitude' to civil rights plaintiffs; the Court determined McEachin contains no such language and admonished counsel.

Plaintiff attributed to Goel v. Bunge the language that a defendant 'may not rely on statements in the documents for the truth of the matter asserted.' The Court found Goel contains no such language and admonished counsel.

cases.name: Randel Edward Page, Jr. v. Apperson Crump

hallucinationItems.description: Appellant relied on a second nonexistent case citation in his motion for summary reversal; the Court's research confirmed the citation does not exist.
Appellant relied on a nonexistent case citation in his motion for summary reversal; the Court's research confirmed the citation does not exist.
Appellant relied on a third nonexistent or misrepresentative case citation in his motion for summary reversal; the Court's research confirmed the citation was erroneous/nonexistent.

cases.name: Ezequiel Cunha de Moura e outro v. Wallace de Moura Silva

hallucinationItems.description: Petition cited an STJ acórdão by number (1654321) and reproduced a passage; court research showed no matching RJ acórdão and identified REsp 1.654.321/MG (and AREsp 1654321 of different origin), concluding the cited passage/acórdão does not exist as presented.

cases.name: Souza v. City of Fitchburg

hallucinationItems.description: Court observed Plaintiff's opposition (ECF No. 8) contained language suggesting use of a generative AI program (text addressing the plaintiff directly and referring to defense counsel), leading the court to warn about AI unreliability in filings.

cases.name: Nicole Olbera, et al. v. Tiara Sykes

hallucinationItems.description: Appellee's pro se brief included a citation to a seemingly non-existent case; the court noted the citation appeared to be fabricated and an editor's note removed links to invalid citations in the official opinion.

cases.name: Edward Reynolds v. Progressive Technologies, Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited multiple district-court decisions to argue that courts routinely strike overlength briefs; the Court found those cited cases either do not exist or are misattributed.

cases.name: Williams v. Chicago Board Of Education

hallucinationItems.description: Response brief included a non-existent quotation attributed to EEOC v. Univ. of Chicago Hospitals; the court found the case contains no such language and called it a 'totally made-up quotation' possibly from generative AI.
Response brief included a purported quotation attributed to Doe v. Village of Arlington Heights that the court found does not exist.

Response brief cited Engquist as supporting a 'class-of-one' equal-protection claim, but the court noted Engquist actually holds class-of-one claims do not apply in the public employment context, so the brief misrepresents the case's holding.

cases.name: Beedemariam Kassaw v. Wal-Mart Corporation

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff's January 2026 memorandum/sur-reply (Dkt. 79) included AI-generated 'hallucinated cases'—fabricated case citations; Court found apparent use of AI and ordered Dkt. 79 stricken.

cases.name: Jacobs v. Payward, Inc.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited "DOE V. SMITH, No. 3:22-cv-01987 (D. Conn. 2023)", which the Court determined to be a hallucinated (nonexistent) case citation and flagged as undermining the credibility of the filings.

cases.name: Megan E. Bryan v. Child Support Enforcement Agency, State of Hawaiʻi

hallucinationItems.description: Petitioner cited non-existent 'State v. Burdett' as a 1981 opinion at 63 Haw. 357; the actual case at that citation is State v. Masaniai, 63 Haw. 354, 628 P.2d 1018 (1981), which does not address family court jurisdiction. Court found the citation fabricated and unsupported.
Petition included four additional inaccurate or non-existent case citations that did not support the propositions for which they were cited; court characterized these as hallucinated citations.

cases.name: Pizzimenti v. City of Detroit, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited Opulent Life Church, 697 F.3d 279 in support of fines/Eighth Amendment/retaliation arguments, but the court noted that Opulent Life Church does not involve fines nor those claims and was thus misapplied.
Plaintiff cited 'Williams v. City of Cleveland, No. 1:18-CV-2911, 2019 WL 5485190 (N.D. Ohio Oct. 25, 2019),' which the court and R&R found does not exist in Westlaw or under that docket.
Plaintiff quoted language attributed to Jensen v. Utah Cnty., 2025 WL 2208257 (D. Utah Aug. 4, 2025), but the quoted passage does not appear in that opinion.
Complaint referenced 'Thaxton v. City of Pontiac, No. 298862, 2011 WL 522951 (Mich. Ct. App. Feb. 15, 2011),' a citation the court says does not exist.
Complaint referenced 'Smith v. State, 26 N.E.3d 1167 (Ind. 2015),' which the court was unable to locate or identify as relevant.

cases.name: Patrick C. Shaltry, I v. Brent Benzing, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff attributed the sentence "A conviction is not final until the completion of the direct appeal." to People v. Swafford; the Court found the quote does not appear in the opinion and noted cited passages contradict the proposition.
Plaintiff attributed the sentence "A judgment is not final while an appeal of right remains pending." to People v. Kennedy; the Court found the quote does not appear in the opinion.

Plaintiff cited "People v. Smith, 489 Mich. 292, 311 (2011)" for the proposition "A conviction pending on direct appeal is not a final adjudication." The Court was unable to locate the cited case and treated the citation as fabricated.

Plaintiff cited "Savin v. City of Cleveland, 2022 WL 3715087 (6th Cir. 2022)"; the Court found the Westlaw citation pointed to an unrelated brief and could not locate a case by that name, indicating a likely mis-citation or fabrication.

cases.name: McCarthy v. U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration

hallucinationItems.description: One cited DEA adjudication in the Opening Brief was entirely fabricated (did not exist); discovered when the Court ordered copies of the cited authorities.

Seven DEA-adjudication summaries were riddled with factual and legal inaccuracies and misrepresented the adjudications (e.g., portrayed lesser sanctions than actually imposed); the summaries were AI-generated and not verified by counsel.

cases.outcome: Reprimand; Notification to other courts and the National Disciplinary Data Bank

cases.name: Amtrust North America o/b/o Justin McGinness v. Liberty Mutual Insurance Company

hallucinationItems.description: Counsel cited this non-existent Appellate Division case to support the carrier's right to subrogation against UIM benefits; court found it does not appear in the NJ Superior Court Reports.
Counsel cited this nonexistent Law Division decision (and purported affirmance) to support subrogation; court found no record of the case in NJ reports.
Counsel cited this purported 2006 Appellate Division case; court determined the cited authority does not exist in the NJ Superior Court Reports.
Counsel cited this alleged 1971 Law Division decision and its purported affirmance; court found no such opinions in the NJ Superior Court Reports.

cases.name: Lauren K. Morgan v. Michael P. Whitticom

hallucinationItems.description: Defendant cited 'Douchette v. Doucette, 50 Conn. App. 541, 544-45 (1998)' for the proposition that property distributions may be modified; court found the reporter citation corresponds to a different opinion (Glass v. Peter Mitchell Construction Leasing and Development Corporation, 50 Conn. App. 539 (1998)) and concluded the citation is an AI-generated hallucination.

Multiple other fictitious or inapplicable cases and legal propositions in the defendant's motion (notably pages 2–3); court found most cited cases do not support the asserted propositions or do not exist.

cases.name: Andrea K. Tantaros v. Fox News Network, LLC, et al.

hallucinationItems.description: Plaintiff cited 'Roe v. Bernabei & Katz, PLLC, 314 F. Supp. 3d 200,209 (D.D.C. 2018)' as analogous authority; Court identified this citation as inaccurate/non-existent.

In the sur-reply, plaintiff inaccurately cited 'Torres v. City of New York, No. 20-CV-3384, 2021 WL 3725985, at 8 (S.D.N.Y. Aug. 23, 2021)' for the proposition that words and threats can constitute actionable violence; Court marked the citation inaccurate.

Plaintiff quoted non-existent statutory language in the GMVA, asserting it 'expressly applies to' assault, sexual misconduct, sexual abuse, and other forms of gender-based violence; Court found the quoted statutory language non-existent.

Plaintiff cited a non-existent case 'M.D. v. New York State Off For People with Developmental Disabilities, 2023 WL 3404357, at *10 (S.D.N.Y. 2023)' to support that verbal, psychological, and physical abuse are actionable under the GMVA; Court identified it as non-existent/inaccurate.
In her sur-reply, plaintiff inaccurately cited 'Garcia v. Comprehensive Ctr., LLC, No. 21-CV-6945, 2022 WL 2237452, at 4-5 (S.D.N.Y. June 22, 2022)'; Court found the citation inaccurate.

Warden of Allenwood Low) that exist but are irrelevant to his claim — the court treated these as misattributed and not supportive of petitioner's position.

cases.name: Whiting v. City of Athens, Tenn.

hallucinationItems.description: Brief cited 'Berg v. Knox Cnty., 2024 WL 2012345, at *4 (6th Cir. Mar. 12, 2024)'; the court could not locate any such Sixth Circuit opinion and the WL citation produced no opinion.

Brief attributed several quoted propositions to Fox v. Vice that do not appear in that opinion; the court found the quoted language absent from Fox.

Brief repeatedly quoted '"[T]he mere fact that a plaintiff did not prevail does not mean that the claim was frivolous."' and attributed it to Adcock-Ladd v. Secretary of the Treasury, but that language does not appear in Adcock-Ladd.

Brief cited 'Jones v. Hamilton Cnty., 29 F.4th 647, 655 (6th Cir. 2022)' for a Sixth Circuit proposition, but the reporter pages cited corresponded to unrelated Tenth Circuit opinions; the Sixth Circuit Jones exists only as an unpublished opinion and was misrepresented.
Brief cited Complaint paragraph (R.31, ¶32) as stating that 'citizens were forced to park on highways and streets,' but the cited paragraph did not contain that statement.

Brief claimed Lindke v. Freed overruled the district court on legislative immunity and that Lindke addressed legislative immunity; the court noted Lindke concerned social-media blocking and was mischaracterized.

cases.outcome: Adverse Costs Order; Monetary Sanction; Potential disciplinary proceedings

cases.name: Joseph Maldonado v. Professional Animal Retirement Center

hallucinationItems.description: Cited a case identifier and Westlaw pin that do not match any order at that date; the named case exists under a different citation and does not support the asserted standing proposition.

Cited FEC v. Akins as supporting that denial of information necessary for scientific work is a recognized injury; Akins discusses standing in the election context and does not support the ESA/data proposition asserted.

Cited WildEarth Guardians for the proposition that denial of information necessary for scientific work constitutes injury, but the decision does not support the ESA-related information-injury argument advanced.

Represented Ringling Bros. as holding that conservation/scientific professionals have standing when mistreatment disrupts their work; the D.C. Cir. opinion actually found the plaintiff's asserted desire to work with elephants speculative and relied on aesthetic-injury authority.
Provided citations that return unrelated matters or cannot be located at the cited identifiers (examples include PETA-related citations that point to different jurisdictions or unrelated cases).

The brief contained 12 quotations (11 fabricated) and numerous inaccurate or inapposite citations (some reporter citations corresponding to different criminal cases); court characterized the brief as 'peppered with inaccurate citations' and AI-generated fabrications.

cases.name: Straub v. Henderson
In re termination of parental rights (HK)

hallucinationItems.description: Mother cited JS-501568, 177 Ariz. at 577, attributing multiple sentences the court could not find, including 'a parent's ability to maintain a relationship must be judged by what they do, not what another parent does or does not do' and 'minimal efforts do not preclude abandonment ... sporadic or infrequent contact is not normal parental contact.' The court concluded these quotes are not in that opinion.

Mother's brief quoted A.R.S. § 8-531(1) as stating: '[a]bandonment is measured by a parent's conduct, not the parent's subjective intent.' The court found this quoted statutory language does not appear in § 8-531(1).

Mother's brief attributed language to A.R.S. § 8-533(B)(1) stating abandonment is established when a parent has failed to maintain a normal parental relationship 'without just cause.' The court found the statute does not contain the quoted phrasing.

Mother's brief attributed a non-existent quote to Raymond F. v. Dep't of Econ. Sec., 224 Ariz. 373, 378, ¶ 21 (App. 2010): '[a] parent who creates barriers to contact through substance abuse cannot claim just cause for his failure to maintain a relationship.' The court found this quotation does not appear in Raymond F.

hallucinationItems.description: Counsel cited United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d 1198 (11th Cir. 2002) as supporting civil-extortion elements; the Court noted Pendergraft addresses criminal extortion under the Hobbs Act, not civil extortion under Ohio law (misapplied authority).

Counsel cited 'State v. Carter, 72 Ohio App. 3d 553 (2d Dist. 1991)' as authority for an element of civil extortion; the court's review found no such opinion supporting that proposition (case appears non-existent/mislinked).
Counsel relied on 'In re Protech, 51 F.4th 714 (6th Cir. 2022)' in briefing; the Court's independent review found no Sixth Circuit opinion matching that citation (phantom citation).

Counsel cited 'Dart Indus. Co. v. Hurd, 66 Ohio St. 2d 280 (1981)' for protection of settlement demand letters; the Court determined that citation was fabricated/misleading and not applicable as asserted.

Counsel cited Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Office Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912 (N.D. Ohio 2011) for a particular pleading/particularity rule for DTSA/OUTSA; Court found the cited passages do not state the proposition relied upon (mischaracterized authority).

Counsel cited 'State ex rel. Ellis v. Cleveland Mun. Sch. Dist., 2015-Ohio-760' as supporting that settlement demand letters are non-actionable; Court found the cited authority was actually a different federal case (309 F. Supp. 2d 1019) and irrelevant to proposition.

Counsel quoted or paraphrased United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55 (2d Cir. 1999) as saying that informing authorities of misconduct cannot be an unlawful threat; the Court found no such language in Jackson and that the opinion does not stand for the asserted proposition.

Counsel relied on 'State v. Milam, 2022-Ohio-3965 (10th Dist.)' for an extortion element; the Court found no such case addressing extortion (citation does not support proposition / appears nonexistent).

cases.name: Kennon v. Ashley

hallucinationItems.description: A block-quoted colloquy attributed to Ashley (including 'what he said, not what he did' and 'It was the language') is quoted as from Doc.55-1 at 33:2-11 but the court found those phrases do not appear at that location or elsewhere in the deposition.

Plaintiff quotes exchanges purportedly showing Ashley answered 'No, not that I recall,' 'There was no contact,' and 'not threatening'; court found those quoted answers do not appear in the cited portions of Ashley's deposition (Doc.55-1 at 107:13-15,108:2-6,109:1-4).

Plaintiff attributes to Spencer a block quote denying any threatening behavior after watching bodycam ('No. I didn't.' / 'He didn't do that'); court found those phrases and the quoted exchange do not appear at Doc.55-2 80:4-6,81:1-3.

Plaintiff quotes Ashley as saying he concluded disorderly conduct 'after reviewing the video footage' (Doc.55-1 at 151:4-9); court found the deposition does not contain 'footage' or the asserted colloquy at the cited location.

Plaintiff attributes to Ashley a short exchange ('No.' / 'So why did you arrest him?' / 'He said, "Take me to jail." I said, "Okay."') citing Doc.55-1 at 116:12-17; court found the quoted exchange as presented does not exist and omits surrounding context that contradicts the representation.
Plaintiff quotes a long passage allegedly from Doc.55-1 at 161:22-162:16; the court found the precise quoted passage does not exist at that citation.

Plaintiff's brief quotes Ashley as admitting the arrest was based solely on 'language' and that there were 'no threatening gestures or movements'; court found the quoted phrase does not appear at Doc.55-1 33:7-11 or elsewhere in the deposition.

Brief asserts Ashley testified he did not determine probable cause until months later after reviewing video; court found the cited portion (Doc.55-1 at 151:4-13) does not support that statement and the word 'footage' does not appear there.

Plaintiff claims both Ashley and Spencer confirmed Kennon made no threatening gestures and 'never chested up' in a disorderly-conduct manner; court found Ashley's cited testimony actually describes chesting up and Spencer's testimony differs, so the quoted summary is inaccurate/nonexistent.

Proposed reply brief cited a nonexistent "Himmelstein v. Comcast of the D.C., LLC, 908 F.3d 49; aff'g 44 F. Supp. 3d 1 (D.D.C. 2012)"—citations that do not exist and were inconsistent with the intended New York Court of Appeals decision.

Response to Order to Show Cause quoted a passage presented as from Mata v. Avianca but the quoted language was actually from a secondary article by Christopher F. Lyon; counsel failed to attribute the quote properly.

Reply brief and subsequent explanations showed misattributed/mismatched reporter citations (e.g., counsel relied on a Google Scholar result for Himmelstein that returned unrelated D.D.C. citations like 931 F. Supp. 2d 48), reflecting AI- or search-induced misrepresentation of the authoritative source.

Plaintiffs included invented quotations and inaccurate summaries attributed to various authorities that the Court could not locate in the cited sources.

cases.name: Elden v HMRC

hallucinationItems.description: Skeleton summarized Atlantic Electronics Ltd v HMRC [2012] UKUT 45 (TCC) as if it were a strike-out authority; Tribunal read the full 9-page decision and found the summary inaccurate and irrelevant (case concerned MTIC VAT and admission of additional evidence).

Skeleton presented Leeds City Council as if it were about denying a full hearing/strike-out; Tribunal read the 11-page decision and found the skeleton's summary misleading (case concerned costs and delay, not strike-out).

Skeleton misdated and mischaracterised Hok Ltd v HMRC (cited as 2011) and presented it as a strike-out authority; Tribunal found this inaccurate (case concerns PAYE penalties, not strike-out) and noted the misattribution.

Passages in the skeleton attributed to 'Hok' were in fact taken from Biguzzi v Rank Leisure plc [1999] 1 WLR 1926 (CA); Tribunal concluded case-heading/citation substitutions had occurred, producing materially misleading summaries.

cases.outcome: Order to include statements of truth about AI use
